requirement: Right-safe real high-speed mantis shrimp strike/cavitation sequence visibly showing strike/contact, cavitation bubble/flash, bubble collapse, and second impact/shock.

vetted_candidates.title: Extreme impact and cavitation forces of a biological hammer: strike forces of the peacock mantis shrimp Odontodactylus scyllarus
vetted_candidates.visual_fit: Excellent lead: article states high-speed imaging, first force peak from limb impact, second force peak from cavitation-bubble collapse, and says videos are available online as supplementary material.
vetted_candidates.reason_blocked: Article/PDF is published by The Company of Biologists 2005; no explicit CC/reuse license found for supplementary videos. Current site blocks automated access with 403/Cloudflare, and legacy DC1 URL returned 403. Cannot use/download as right-safe production material without permission or explicit license.

blocker: No right-safe real high-speed mantis shrimp strike/cavitation/bubble-collapse/second-impact source was found in scientific supplements, university lab pages, Wikimedia Commons, Internet Archive, Vimeo, or YouTube metadata checks. The strongest real footage exists as scientific/publisher supplements or platform clips, but every located real-footage candidate lacks explicit reuse rights or has NC/ND restrictions. Therefore WAYS Gate 2 should remain blocked for real-source footage.
best_next_leads: Request written permission from Sheila Patek/Patek Lab and/or The Company of Biologists for the JEB 2005 supplementary videos for social short reuse; this is the best scientific match for first impact + cavitation collapse/second force peak.
Request permission/license quote from BIOPIXEL for Vimeo 122396213; visually likely closest accessible clip, but current public page is not reusable.

If permission is not available, proceed only with the already-approved fallback lane: a clearly labeled deterministic motion-graphic proof sequence showing club impact, bubble formation, bubble collapse, and second shock. Do not present it as real footage.
Keep Wikimedia Commons CC BY/CC BY-SA mantis stills or mechanics GIFs only as supporting/context visuals; they do not satisfy the cavitation proof by themselves.
